conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
You've been smoking bidis for 45 years and have managed to reduce the number a bit. What are some situations or emotions that make you reach for a bidi, even though you're trying to cut down?
When I wake up in the morning, it's hard for me to resist. Also, when I'm stressed about my farm and I make mistakes. Sometimes when I'm bored during slow field days, I feel like I need one.

I hear that mornings are a challenging time for you, and also when you're stressed about the farm or feeling bored on slow field days. Would it be helpful to try a morning routine that replaces your bidi habit, like taking a short walk or doing some stretching exercises?
That might be helpful, but it's hard to start a new routine in the morning. What if I don't feel like walking or stretching?
That's a common concern. What if you just committed to a very small, one-minute step, like making your bed or drinking a glass of water, as soon as you wake up?
Making my bed is easy, I can do that. But how is that going to stop me from smoking?

I understand you're wondering how making your bed will stop you from smoking. What do you think would need to happen for you to feel like making your bed is a helpful start to the day, even if it doesn't completely eliminate your craving for a bidi?
I suppose if I do the bed thing first, I might be able to wait a little longer before having a bidi, and maybe that can help with the morning craving.

Now that you think making your bed can help, let's think about how we can make it a consistent habit. What specific times of the morning do you think you'll make your bed, so it becomes a part of your daily routine?
Right after I wake up, before I even get out of bed, I'll make it. That way, it'll be the first thing I do and it'll become a habit I guess.
Now that you've committed to making your bed right after waking up, what's the first thing you'll do if you find yourself reaching for a bidi after completing this new morning habit?
I think I'll just put the bidi back and get out of here, start doing some work on the farm. It's not like I have time for a bidi anyway, if I want to get everything done.

Based on the patient's recent conversation, it seems like they're starting to develop a plan to manage their morning craving. Now that they've thought through making their bed and then putting the bidi back, are you open to exploring other ways to occupy your hands and distract yourself from the craving while you work on the farm?
Yes that's right, I can try to find something else to do with my hands, maybe carry some tools or do some other small task. What do you think would be good?

Let's work with the idea of using physical activity to distract yourself from cravings. Would you be willing to experiment with a short, manageable task, like carrying a basket of tools or water, to see if it helps you stay occupied and focused on your work?
Okay, I can try carrying a basket or something when I go to check on the crops, see if it helps. But what if I still want a bidi after I'm done?

Now that you're considering carrying a basket as a distraction, can you think of any other activities or tasks you could do while checking on the crops that might make carrying a basket even more helpful?
I can take a small notebook with me to record some notes on the crops, or check the water levels for the plants. If I'm busy writing or checking the water, I might not think about the bidi as much.

Now that you're thinking of ways to keep your hands and mind occupied while checking on the crops, it sounds like writing in a small notebook might be a good fit - would you be willing to start carrying it with you every time you go to check the water?
Yes, I'll try that. It's not a big deal to carry a notebook around, and it might actually help me keep track of things better on the farm.
I'm so confident you'll find this approach helpful and supportive in your daily farm work.
I'm hopeful it will work. The doctor here seems to know what he's talking about, and I'm tired of feeling sick and tired all the time. Maybe this is the start of something good.

It sounds like you're feeling hopeful and motivated, and you mentioned being tired of feeling sick and tired all the time. How do you think carrying a notebook with you to the crops will fit into your morning routine, which you mentioned is a time when you often crave a bidi?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
It sounds like you've been smoking bidis for a significant amount of time, and it's become a daily habit for you. Can you tell me, what time of day or situation usually prompts you to smoke a bidi?
When my coworkers are on a break or when we're on a site and I see them smoking, they often offer me one and say "have one, yaar". It becomes a habit then.

It sounds like you feel a strong sense of social pressure to smoke when your coworkers are around. Can you tell me, how do you think quitting smoking would affect your relationships with your coworkers?
They'll probably think I'm strange if I don't smoke when they do. They won't even notice, I think.

It sounds like you're concerned about how your coworkers might react if you don't smoke with them. What does that thought make you feel, and is that concern strong enough for you to consider quitting?
It makes me feel a bit awkward, I guess. But it's not strong enough to make me want to quit. I've tried before, but it doesn't seem like it's going to make a big difference in my life, so why bother?

You're feeling like quitting might not make a big difference in your life, and that's a tough feeling to overcome. What is it that you think would need to change for quitting smoking to feel like it's worth it to you?

If quitting really helped my wife with our household expenses, that's when I might think about it more seriously. Financially, we're really struggling, and every rupee counts. If I could really see a way that quitting would help us out, maybe then I'd consider it.

You think that quitting smoking would make a big difference if it helped your wife with our household expenses. Considering that, I'd like to try something called a "visualization exercise" - would you be open to imagining how your life would be like 3 months from now if you had already quit smoking and seen some positive changes in your finances?
I'll try it. What do I need to do?
I appreciate your willingness to try this exercise, and I'm looking forward to hearing how it goes.
We can try it now. But, what's the point of imagining if it's not going to happen in real life?

You're feeling skeptical about the value of a visualization exercise, and it's natural to wonder if it's just a fantasy that won't translate to real life. How might you make this exercise more tangible and connected to your real-life goals, like helping your wife with household expenses?

If I can see a specific way that quitting will make our lives better, like being able to afford a new bike for my daughter or something, then maybe it'll feel more real. And maybe then I can start planning how to make that happen.

Let's take the idea of visualizing a specific, positive outcome like affording a new bike for your daughter. Now that you've thought about what that would be like, can you imagine the different steps you might take to make that a reality, like finding ways to reduce household expenses or increasing income?

I could try to reduce the expenses on things like food and rent. Maybe my wife and I could make do with simpler meals, or see if we can negotiate a lower rent with the landlord. And as for increasing income, I could try to take up more work at the construction site or maybe even start working on some side jobs during the weekends. But, it's easier said than done.

You're feeling like taking action is just easier said than done, and that's a common challenge when it comes to making significant changes in our lives. Can you think of one small step you could take this week to start working on reducing household expenses?
Maybe I could start writing down all our expenses for the week and see where we can cut back. It's not a big step, but it's something, I guess.

Now that you've thought of starting to track your expenses, are you feeling more confident that taking this small step will help you stay motivated to work towards your goal of affording a new bike for your daughter?
Yeah, I guess so. It's not like I'm committing to quitting smoking or anything, just starting to keep track of our expenses. That feels doable.

Now that you feel more confident about tracking your expenses, let's talk about how that might relate to your smoking habits - in situations where you tend to smoke, like with friends, what would be one thing you could do differently this time around?
I could say no when they offer me a bidi. Just that.

You're feeling hopeful that simply saying no to the bidi offer could be a small but effective step in resisting social triggers. How do you think you would feel if you were to practice saying no a few times in low-stakes situations, like with a store clerk or a friend who's not smoking, before trying it with people who smoke?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
You've been smoking bidis and eating paan for about 7-8 years now. What motivated you to start smoking bidis back when you were 25?
It was a habit my friends had, and I guess I started because I wanted to be like them. We would have these gatherings and smoke bidis, so I just sort of... got used to it.

You wanted to be like your friends back then, and it seems that social gatherings where you'd smoke bidis together were a big part of that. What role do you think paan plays in your smoking habit now, is it a ritual or a companion to your bidis?

Paan is... I eat it because it's a habit now, I think. If I'm eating bidis, I'll often have a paan too. I'm trying to cut down on paan, but it's hard. My daughter says it's like a cycle, one thing leads to another, and I should try to break the cycle.

You're starting to see that there's a connection between your bidis and paan, and that breaking the cycle might be key to making progress. Have you considered trying to break the paan-eating habit by finding a different, healthier activity to do while you're trying to quit bidis?

Finding something else to do sounds good, but... I don't know, I'm just sitting at home most of the day, watching TV, or sleeping. I don't really have anything to keep myself busy. It's just... lonely.

You're feeling a bit lonely, and that's something we can work with. Would you be open to trying some small, creative activities that don't require a lot of energy, like drawing or listening to music, to start filling that time?

Drawing sounds okay, I guess. I used to listen to music a lot, but it's not the same anymore. It makes me think of my wife, and she loved listening to music together. I don't know... it's all just too much.

You mentioned that listening to music right now is hard because it reminds you of your wife. Is there a way we could explore other types of music, or even sounds, that might not trigger those sad feelings, and could help you find some comfort in the silence?

Maybe that could help. But it's hard to think about finding music that doesn't remind me of her. We had so many songs that we used to listen to together... I just don't know if I can find anything else that feels the same.

You're feeling stuck on the music that reminds you of your wife, and it's hard to imagine finding something else that feels the same. Would you consider looking through your old playlists together and finding one song, or a moment, that doesn't feel as painful, and start from there?
Maybe that's a start. One song, yeah... I think I can do that. But it's hard to go through the old playlists, it just brings back memories... of all of it.

You're feeling overwhelmed by the memories that come up when looking through old playlists. Would you be willing to try labeling or acknowledging those memories as they arise, rather than getting caught up in them, to help you stay present with the task at hand?
I'll try... It's just hard to let go. Memories of my wife, they're still so fresh. I feel like I'm moving forward, but at the same time, I'm just stuck in the past.

You're feeling stuck between moving forward and holding onto the past, and it's making it hard to let go of memories. Would you consider keeping a small, portable journal to write down your thoughts and feelings when you start to feel overwhelmed, to help you process and release them more easily?
A journal might be okay. But what if I write all these things down and then just end up feeling worse?

You feel that writing down your thoughts and feelings might make you feel worse, which is a valid concern. Would you be willing to try writing down just one thing you're grateful for each day, in addition to your thoughts and feelings, to help balance out your focus?
Maybe that could help. My daughters are always looking out for me, so maybe I could write about that. Yeah, that sounds like something I could do.

With the idea of writing down one thing you're grateful for each day resonating with you, what form would this journal take - a physical notebook or a note-taking app on your phone - that would make it more likely for you to stick to it?
I think a small notebook would be better. I don't want to be on my phone all day, it's just another thing to distract me. A notebook feels... more personal, I guess.

You think a small notebook would be better, and it feels more personal. Are you willing to make a commitment to carrying that notebook with you every day, so you can write down your thoughts, feelings, and gratitude as soon as you feel the need to?
Yeah, I can do that. I'll keep a notebook with me. Today's my first day, right?
You're taking this first step with courage and intention, and I'm here to support you along the way.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
You're coming in here because your son is being treated, and it's making you think about your own smoking. How long have you been smoking, if you don't mind me asking?
About 12 years now. I'm a teacher, so it's been tough.

Given your history as a teacher, I'm guessing you're no stranger to managing stress. Do you think having a morning routine that helps you transition from a relaxed state to a more focused one could help you avoid those morning cravings?
I'm not sure, I usually just grab a smoke to wake myself up. It helps calm me down too, like a bad habit.

It sounds like having a morning routine could be a helpful thing to explore, but it's not a replacement for addressing that underlying need for calm. 

Can you imagine what a morning without smoking might look like for you, and what you think might help you feel more grounded and ready for the day?
To be honest, I don't know. My life's pretty routine already - wake up, make coffee, have a smoke. I guess I'd miss that first smoke, but it can't be that hard to quit, right?

You think it can't be that hard to quit, but you're still struggling to imagine a morning without your first smoke. What do you think would be the most difficult part of giving up that first cigarette for you?

I think it's just the feeling of being unable to control my anger when it hits. I get frustrated with the kids, and then I smoke to calm down. It's like a safety valve, you know? Without that, I'm not sure I'd know how to deal with my anger.

I think I understand that feeling of being overwhelmed by anger. It sounds like your first cigarette serves as a way to calm down and regain control when things get tough. Have you ever stopped to think about what happens after you've calmed down with that first cigarette - how do you usually handle the rest of the morning with your kids?

After I've smoked, I'm usually okay. I'll go into class, teach, the day goes on... But I've noticed my wife is always on my case about smoking too much. She says I'm a bad example for our son. Maybe that's part of why I'm really here, not just because of the doctor's appointment.

You feel like you can function okay after having that first cigarette, but it sounds like there's a nagging feeling from your wife about being a bad example for your son. How does that weight of being a role model for your son make you feel, and does it motivate you to quit smoking in a way that's different from your doctor's appointment and weight concerns?

It's hard to explain...I guess it feels like I'm letting my son down, and that's worse than letting my wife down or even myself down. He looks up to me, and I want to be the kind of person he can respect and follow.

You're really struggling with the idea of being a good role model for your son, and that's giving you a strong motivation to quit smoking. What do you think would happen if instead of reaching for a cigarette, you took a few deep breaths and tried to stay present with your kids when they're driving you crazy?

I don't know...it sounds so easy to say, but I've tried before with my wife, and it doesn't work. I get so angry and impatient, and I just feel like exploding. I don't know how to just breathe it out and calm down like that.

You've expressed concerns that deep breathing might not work for you given your past experiences. Can you imagine a scenario where you're feeling overwhelmed, but instead of trying deep breathing, you simply acknowledge and accept those feelings, without judgment, before reacting?

I guess so...like, I'm feeling angry, and I just say to myself, "I'm feeling angry right now." It sounds stupid, but I've never done that before. I've always tried to fix it or get away from it, or...or smoke. But just acknowledging it...I don't know, maybe it's worth a try.

You've expressed a willingness to try acknowledging your emotions, which is a great step forward. Can you imagine practicing this simple acknowledgment with a specific situation, like when you're feeling frustrated with your kids, and seeing how it might change your reaction to them?

Yeah, I can try. Last week, my son was being really annoying, and I got angry and yelled at him. Maybe next time, I'll just say to myself, "I'm feeling angry, I'm feeling frustrated." And then...then what? Do I just sit there and feel it, or can I still do the same things, like teaching them or...or yelling?

You're feeling more hopeful about trying to acknowledge your emotions before reacting. Can you think of one specific situation, like teaching your kids, where you'd like to try this new approach and see how it might work for you?

I think it would be when my son is misbehaving in class. I always get frustrated with him, and I yell at him. I want to try saying to myself, "I'm feeling frustrated, I'm feeling angry," before I react. Maybe I can take a deep breath, or count to ten, or something, and then deal with him calmly. That would be a good place to start.

I hear you want to try acknowledging your emotions before reacting in a specific situation, like with your son misbehaving in class. Can you imagine how incorporating a brief pause, like taking a deep breath or counting to ten, might help you stay calm and respond more thoughtfully?

Yeah, it makes sense. If I can just pause and breathe, maybe I can think more clearly and come up with a better way to handle the situation. It's not about yelling or losing control, it's about being a better teacher and role model for my son. That's the real thing.

You're feeling more hopeful about using the pause and breath technique to handle situations with your son. Now that we have a clear idea of what you'd like to try, let's talk about how you can apply this in real-life situations. What's the first step you can take to start practicing this new approach, for example, setting aside a few minutes each day to practice your deep breathing?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about being here, and you think smoking isn't a big issue for you. Can you remember a recent time when you wished you hadn't smoked?
No, not really. My wife sometimes gets on my case about it, but it's not like I've had a specific time when smoking did some damage to me.

It sounds like you're not really feeling the urgency to quit smoking from your own perspective, but rather from your wife's concerns. What do you think might change for you if you were to overcome this habit on your own, without your wife's pressure?

I guess it's hard to imagine right now, but if I'm being honest, it might be nice to have a little more energy when I'm opening up the shop in the morning. Sometimes I feel a bit winded doing the same things. But it's just habit at this point.
It sounds like you feel a bit winded doing the same things, especially in the morning. Can you describe what your day is like, from when you wake up to when you open the shop?

It's pretty routine. Wake up, have a cup of tea, take a quick walk outside to get some air, then head to the shop. Open it up, tidy things up a bit, and then just kind of wait for customers to start coming in. Sometimes it can get a bit slow, and that's when I usually reach for a cigarette.

It sounds like your morning routine sets you up for a potential smoking trigger when the shop gets slow. Can you think of a small, achievable change you could make to your morning routine that might reduce your urge to smoke?

Actually, now that you mention it, I do have a small notebook where I jot down notes and reminders. I could probably start doing some simple math problems or drawing in that notebook while I'm waiting for customers. That way I might be less likely to grab a cigarette.

That's a great idea to use your notebook to distract yourself from smoking when the shop gets slow. Are you open to trying this notebook idea out for the next few days and seeing if it helps you stay smoke-free?
Yeah, I suppose it couldn't hurt to try. Maybe it'll make a difference. But what if it doesn't work? I don't want to feel like I'm trying something that's not going to help.

You're worried that this might not work, and that's a valid concern. What if we were to make this notebook idea just one part of a larger plan to help you manage your mornings - can you imagine what else you could do to make your morning routine even more engaging and smoke-free?

Well, I've always thought about listening to music while I'm opening up the shop, but I never actually get around to it. Maybe I could try listening to some music or the radio, and see if that makes the time pass a bit faster.

You're considering listening to music or the radio to make your mornings more engaging. Would you be willing to start with just a 15-minute music session each morning and see if that helps shift your focus away from smoking?
Okay, yeah, that sounds manageable. I can do 15 minutes. But can I just pick the music myself, or do I have to stick to some specific station or playlist?
You'd like to choose the music yourself, that's great - can you imagine what kind of music would actually help you get in a good mood and feel more energized at the start of your day?
You know, I used to really like Bollywood music, but I haven't listened to it in ages. Maybe that's something I could put back on in the mornings. It always makes me want to get moving.

You're thinking of getting back into Bollywood music, which always energized you in the past. Would you be willing to actually put it on every morning for the next week, starting with those 15 minutes we discussed, and see how it helps shift your focus away from smoking?
Alright, yeah, I'll give it a shot. But if it doesn't work out, I'm blaming you for making me try this silly music thing.
I'm so glad you're willing to give it a try, and I'll be rooting for you every step of the way.
Thanks, I guess it couldn't hurt to try something new. I'm still not convinced I'll actually quit, but I'll at least try not to smoke as much.
Now that you're willing to try something new, what specific goals do you hope to achieve with reducing your cigarette smoking, and how will you measure success for yourself?

I guess I'd be happy if I could cut down to just 5 or 6 cigarettes a day. That's a smaller number, right? And I could try to track it by keeping a log of how many I smoke each day. That way I'll have some idea of whether I'm actually making progress.
Now that we have a goal of reducing cigarettes to 5 or 6 a day, how do you think keeping a log and playing Bollywood music in the morning might work together to help you reach that goal?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

It sounds like you're feeling really discouraged about trying to quit smoking again, and you're not sure why you're even coming to see me. Can you tell me more about what happens when you do try to quit - what do you usually experience, and what makes it so hard to stick with it?

It's always the same thing. I get withdrawal headaches and I'm so anxious. And I get depressed. It's like, I'm just miserable all around. And the cravings are unbearable. But it's not just that - when I try to quit, I gain so much weight. That's the biggest fear for me, weight gain.

You're mentioning a lot of uncomfortable physical and emotional symptoms that come up when you try to quit, and weight gain is a big concern for you. Can you tell me more about what you think drives your desire to smoke when you're feeling anxious or depressed?

It's just... it's like my mind is constantly searching for something to calm me down, and smoking's always been that thing. I know it sounds stupid, but it's like an old friend to me. When I'm stressed at work or bored at home, it's just... I don't know, it's just there, you know?

I sense that smoking has become a deeply ingrained habit for you, almost like a comfort in difficult moments. Have you ever thought about replacing smoking with another activity that brings you a similar sense of calm, like taking a few deep breaths or going for a short walk?

I've tried deep breathing exercises and stuff before, but it's just not the same. And walking, yeah, I like walking, but it's not like smoking is something I can just switch out for something else. It's like, I'm not smoking because I like the taste or anything, it's just... it's just what I do. And when I'm not doing it, I feel like I'm missing something.

It sounds like you feel like you're missing something when you're not smoking, and that's a big part of why it's hard for you to quit. 

Can you think of anything that might help fill that void when you wake up in the morning, which you mentioned is a trigger for you?

I guess I just wish I could find something that's as easy as grabbing my pack of cigarettes and stepping outside for a smoke. Like, I don't want to have to think about it, I don't want to have to plan it out, I just want to be able to do it without thinking. But at the same time, I know that's not healthy.

You're looking for a quick and effortless way to fill the void, something that's as simple as grabbing a cigarette. Have you considered carrying a small stress ball or fidget toy with you, something that can be a discreet and easy-to-reach comfort when you're feeling anxious or bored?

I don't know... a stress ball or something like that doesn't seem like it would really do the trick. I mean, it's not like I'm just missing a physical thing, it's... it's just this feeling of relief that I get from smoking. And I don't know if anything's going to fill that feeling.

You're really struggling to find a replacement for the feeling of relief you get from smoking. Have you ever thought about exploring other activities that might bring you a similar sense of calm, such as listening to soothing music or taking a warm bath?

Maybe. I don't know. It's not like I haven't tried relaxation techniques before. But with smoking, it's just... it's not like it's about relaxation, it's about getting through a moment. Like, when I'm stressed at work, I need something to calm me down right now, you know? Soaking in a bath might take too long, and I don't know about music, it's not really something I've ever thought about.

You're feeling like relaxation techniques aren't quite quick enough to help you get through a stressful moment at work. Have you thought about exploring shorter, more immediate activities, like taking a few deep breaths in through your nose and out through your mouth, or going for a short walk around the block?

I guess that makes sense. I mean, yeah, taking a few deep breaths could be quicker than taking a bath. But it's just... I don't know if it would really help in the moment. Like, when I'm feeling anxious, I need something more immediate, you know?

You're saying that taking a few deep breaths might not be immediate enough to help you in a stressful moment. What if you were to try a tiny, quick activity that you can do with your hands, like doodling or playing with a pen tip, to see if it can help distract you from your anxiety and give you a moment to breathe?

I can try that, I guess. But I don't know, I've tried doodling before and it didn't really help. I mean, it's just something to do with my hands, but it's not like it changed how I was feeling. Still, I can try it, I don't have much else to lose at this point.
You've tried doodling before and didn't find it helpful, but you're willing to try it again. What do you think might be different this time that could help it be more effective for you?

I don't know... I guess maybe the difference would be that I'm trying it for a specific reason, like to help me quit smoking. And maybe if I'm more focused on that goal, it'll make it more effective. Or maybe I just need to find the right thing to doodle, like something calming or soothing. I don't know, I'm just grasping at straws at this point.

You think being focused on your goal to quit smoking might make doodling more effective this time. Are you open to trying a specific doodling pattern that's been found to be calming for some people, like a simple spiral design?

I suppose it couldn't hurt to try. A spiral design sounds easy enough. But you have to promise me it's not going to be like all those other "just try this" thingies that I've tried before. It really needs to help me in a significant way.

I completely understand your skepticism, and I'm not promising a magic solution. What would it take for you to feel confident that trying this spiral design doodling pattern could actually help you manage your cravings and stress?

I guess if you were willing to work with me on a plan to gradually reduce my smoking and increase my doodling and other relaxation techniques, I might feel more confident that it could actually help. Like, if we could come up with a specific schedule and timeline and stuff, that would make me feel more secure. But just telling me to try doodling isn't going to cut it.

Let's create a plan that's tailored to your needs and goals. What specific areas of your life would you like to focus on first - reducing your smoking at work or finding ways to manage stress and cravings in general?
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
It sounds like the dhaba environment makes it tough for you to avoid smoking. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
I guess around 8-10 during my shift and a few more when I'm done and meeting friends at the tea stall. It's just a habit, you know?

It sounds like smoking is deeply ingrained in your daily routine, especially when you're socializing with friends at the tea stall. How do you feel when you're not smoking with your friends - does it make you feel left out or uncomfortable?
Yeah, maybe a little bit. They all smoke and it's just something we do together. If I don't, I feel like I don't fit in, you know?
It sounds like you feel a strong sense of belonging when you're smoking with your friends. Are you worried that quitting smoking would mean losing that sense of connection with them?
I guess so, yeah. What's the point of quitting if I'm just going to be this odd guy who used to hang out with them? I mean, it's not just about quitting, it's about losing my identity, a little.

You're feeling like your identity is tied up in your smoking habit and that quitting would mean losing a part of who you are. What do you think your life would be like if you could find a new sense of identity and connection with your friends that didn't involve smoking?

Honestly, I don't know. Maybe it would be cool, I don't know how to get there, but I guess it's worth thinking about. Can I really hang out with the guys without cigarettes? Would they even want to, without it?

It sounds like you're worried about how your friends will react if you quit smoking, and that's making it hard for you to imagine a life without cigarettes. Can you think of a specific time when your friends were okay with you not smoking, or when they've been supportive of your goals?

Actually, there was this one time my friend Rohan was studying for exams and we were all sitting at the tea stall, he didn't smoke with us because he was trying to stay focused. We still hung out, still talked, it was fine. Maybe that's a good example... but it was just a one-time thing.

You're thinking that maybe having a supportive friend like Rohan who didn't smoke with them is a good sign. Can you imagine how it would feel to have a whole group of friends like that, who are supportive of your goal to quit smoking?

Yeah, that would be really cool, I guess. It would make it easier, you know? Like, I wouldn't feel like the odd one out. But it's not like I can just tell them all to quit with me, that's not how it works. They're my friends, they like smoking too.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck between wanting to quit smoking and not wanting to be different from your friends. Can you think of a situation where you've wanted to hang out with your friends but didn't end up smoking with them?

I think so... there was this one night, we were all going to this concert, and we decided to go for a walk after it ended, just chill and talk.
